provenance.source_corpus: Assessing instream habitat suitability and hydraulic signatures of geomorphic units in a reconstructed single thread meandering channel

source_spans.text: Human activities within a watershed, such as agriculture, forestry and urbanization, impair or alter aquatic ecosystems
Human activities within a watershed, such as agriculture, forestry and urbanization, impair or alter aquatic ecosystems
This impairment is especially evident when streams and channels are moved or modi ﬁed to utilize the land surface around that stream
Channels that were once abundant in ﬁsh species now have reduced populations
Stream restoration projects aim to undo damage and in many cases seek to improve aquatic habitat where possible
Stream restoration projects aim to undo damage and in many cases seek to improve aquatic habitat where possible
at high- ﬂow conditions the boundaries between geomorphic units blurred
at high- ﬂow conditions the boundaries between geomorphic units blurred
During higher- ﬂow events, water depth-velocity distribution for rif ﬂes at high- ﬂow events were such that they took on the hydraulic properties typical of runs
During higher- ﬂow events, water depth-velocity distribution for rif ﬂes at high- ﬂow events were such that they took on the hydraulic properties typical of runs
distinctive hydraulic signatures blur, most notably rif ﬂe habitat decreases
Emery et al. (2003); measured velocity at different geomorphic units at low and high- ﬂows and found geomorphic units were indistinguishable with an increase in water depth
Emery et al. (2003); measured velocity at different geomorphic units at low and high- ﬂows and found geomorphic units were indistinguishable with an increase in water depth
several studies recognize that changes following high- ﬂow events are to be expected
several studies recognize that changes following high- ﬂow events are to be expected
the impacts of siltation on minnow species is well-known to negatively impact feeding and spawning
the impacts of siltation on minnow species is well-known to negatively impact feeding and spawning
redside dace populations were not observed
creek chub was commonly observed throughout the ﬁeld season
Other minnow species were observed during the ﬁeld season including western blacknose dace
Design elements that provided habitat for redside dace include vegetation plantings
Design elements that provided habitat for redside dace include vegetation plantings
if a restoration project builds habitat heterogeneity, increased species diversity should follow
if a restoration project builds habitat heterogeneity, increased species diversity should follow
although the restored channels maintained restored features (e.g. habitat heterogeneity), species diversity did not improve
eggs cannot survive high velocity conditions by seeking refuge
eggs cannot survive high velocity conditions by seeking refuge
Koster (1939) found the lowest temperature, redside dace were observed, and spawning was at 18 °C
Koster (1939) found the lowest temperature, redside dace were observed, and spawning was at 18 °C
During the 2013 study period, 5% of the water temperature measurements in the restored reach exceeded 24 °C
During the 2013 study period, 5% of the water temperature measurements in the restored reach exceeded 24 °C
This was due to diurnal water temperature ﬂuctuations from 15 to 29 May
Approximately, 52% of the water temperature measurements were less than 18 °C
Rif ﬂes were described as ‘drowned’ during high- ﬂows
Rif ﬂes were described as ‘drowned’ during high- ﬂows
